speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December 5, 1996. Survivor is Irene Baruch Gregory. Interviewer is Howard Felson. The city is Oakland, California. The country is United States of America. The language is English. My name is Howard Felson, H-O-W-A-R-D F-E-L-S-O-N. The date is December 5th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Irene Gregory in Oakland, California, United States of America. Would you please say your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Irene I. Gregory. Irene-- I-R-E-N-E-- initial I, Gregory-- G-R-E-G-O-R-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth? And would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baruch-- B-A-R-U-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first name was Ilse, I-L-S-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Ille, a diminutive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-L-L-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your city of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bielefeld, I think. It might have been Herford, but I think it was Bielefeld. They're next door to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bielefeld is B-I-E-L-E-F-E-L-D. And Herford is H-E-R-F-O-R-D, Westphalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 6th, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your age today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your place where you grew up? Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the fir-- first five years, my family lived in Herford. My father had a job as a, a legal adviser to Elsbach and Company. E-L-S-B-A-C-H and Company. And at age five, 1927, we moved to Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of company was your fathers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elsbach was a factory that produced men's shirts, pajamas, underwear. The whole area is-- they produce men's clothing. They produce furniture-- to this day. They make kitchens there. It's a very productive area of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name? And where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Bernard Baruch and he was born there, in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marie Adrienne Baruch, born Teblee, T-E-B-L-E-E-- E, accent aigu.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is she from?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Cologne, on the Rhine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where your grandparents were from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that my father's father was from Hamburg. And his first name was Isidore, and his wife was Jennie Vorreufer. They lived as a married couple in Herford. My grandfather was the Director of Elsbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- my grandfather on my mother's side was Ernst Teblee. Wife's name was Clotilde. That's my grandmother. I believe they resided in Cologne, as far as I know. Could be-- it could be that they-- that she came from somewhere else-- but maybe Frankfurt. Her maiden name was Frank, so I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she work when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother? My mother was a chemist and apparently a good one. I found all kinds of wonderful letters about her. But she never continued to work after she married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a brother who's alive, lives in Morristown, New Jersey. His name is John Baruch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1926. He just turned 70.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school in Herford?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was too young. That started in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about Herford?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I, in fact, was there not too long ago. The city invited all its Jews to come and visit. So Herford did not suffer too much damage. So the city's fairly intact. And what I remembered was my grandfather's house especially, on Augusta Street. Our house was bombed out. And it's a parking lot. And the Elsbach site of then is now a post office. So Herford is-- it's situated in a very beautiful area, really-- hilly, gentle, sloping, and neat and clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your grandfather's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it was very vast. It had a beautiful glass addition. And they were growing flowers and took great pride in their garden, had a, a lovely lifestyle. I remember that house better than the one I grew up in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about their lifestyle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lifestyle-- it was what we might call here upper middle class. You know, there were servants. Life was good. The men worked, the women stayed home or played tennis and piano. Were members of the community. Herford had a sizable Jewish community. There is a cemetery. And I think they were highly respected merchants, for the most part.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your grandfather do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the Director of Elsbach and Company, which manufactured men's shirts, et cetera, for many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember ever celebrating holidays at his house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, absolutely. It was a gay house. We had lots of fun. Jewish holidays were observed. But they weren't people that went to synagogue. They were not-- they observed the holidays. And that, I do have some memory of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mostly I remember the festivity was food. And Jewish holidays are nicely celebrated by proper foods for every occasion. Lovely clothing, lots of friends, enough family-- Very, wonderful life they must have had. I met one of the maids on my visit to Herford and she gave me a little handkerchief she embroidered for my grandmother. But my grandmother died when she was a young woman. And my grandfather gave it back to her to keep, and now I have it. And it's very dear to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate or observe Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe so. They did not light candles on Friday night, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your house like? How many people lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have much recollection, really. My father, my mother, my brother, and I. And also comfortable lifestyle that permitted my mother to live as a society matron, is probably what you would call it now. And she had married her Prince Charming. She was a musician, a tennis player. Her first fiance-- before she met my father, her fiance was killed in the First World War. And so when my-- they had a very romantic marr-- wedding, actually. My father was about to marry someone else. And they were marching down the aisle and all of a sudden, something stopped him. He turned away, and ran out and went straight to Cologne and got my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if it hadn't been for that, I would have had a different mother. So that's way back. Very nice. They had a good marriage. Do you know why he did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why did he do it? Well, it could be that the first marriage was arranged. I don't know. He's not here for me to ask. But I find that very nice. It's really a nice touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why you moved from Herford?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I think my father wanted to be on his own. He wanted to be his own man. So there were opportunities in Berlin that he couldn't find in Herford. Herford wasn't that large.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he came to Berlin, he started a business-- it was a vending machine business. I don't think it lasted very long. It went bankrupt. But then came his life's work. And that was a lovely store near the Reich's Chancellery on Mohrenstrasse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was called [? Harris ?] and he made-- manufactured shirts. Everything was custom and very high style, very high class. So high class that we children weren't allowed in there. I remember the polished floors and the Persian rugs. And it wasn't a place for children-- intimidating.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your house like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived-- the house I grew up in was-- I grew up in a flat. Right-- the street was a cul-de-sac planted with a rose bed in the middle, beautifully maintained rose bed. The houses were turn-of-the-century. They each had-- the buildings were attached to each other like big-- Berlin was a beautiful residential city. The apartments were two to a floor with an elevator in the middle and about nine rooms each on a floor. The end of-- one end of the street was Schoneberg Stadtpark. And that is where Kennedy gave his speech. It also had the city hall nearby. Very gracious-- it's a subway stop. Very sophisticated part of town. It was West Berlin. When I went back to Berlin, my house was gone. And they built-- rebuilt it, not so nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which floor you lived on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Third floor, oh yes. It was on the top floor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember my father giving two rooms to the neighbor. They had a good neighborly situation. That man had an extensive collection of paintings and needed two more rooms. And we had ample. I mean, that flat was bigger than the house that I bought in California. High ceilings, old style-- magnificent, really. Nice place to grow up. Nothing wrong with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, mother, my brother and I, and a servant-- a cook. She had her own quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Martha Bockhaus, I believe. We wrote it down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- do you remember any celebrations at that flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of parties. My parents were young, and there was enough money. At least, once a month, if not more often, there were music evenings. They liked having soirees where people came and talked about books, about happenings. One of their close friends was Leo Baeck. I remember distinctly him as a neighbor. There were many distinguished people that came through there. And we children were never allowed to be up. We had to go to bed. My father was a patriarch. He didn't believe in children running around at night. And we did not speak unless spoken to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which language was spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. No Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of hobbies or activities did you have then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents?

speaker: interviewer
text: You.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just a little kid. I was growing up. My favorite sport was swimming. And I was crushed when I could no longer use the swimming pool.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- when could you not use the swimming pool?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1933, I was 10 years old, and Hitler came to power. The first things he did is he segregated Jews from everybody else. So the place where I worked out-- I was on a team-- was no longer allowed for me to use. It was heartbreaking. And a lot of other things followed. And little by little, paranoia seeped into our bodies, really. You never-- maybe when you were sound asleep, you didn't know you were paranoid. But the rest of any waking moment, there was a lot of paranoia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were younger, do you remember having newspapers, books in your home, any kind of literature?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very literary home. My father was proud of his academic education. And I also remember thinking later-- many years later-- that this kind of society probably died out. Although there is some repetition now in the United States. But they had reached a pinnacle of culture that was unimaginable and enjoyed it, made the most of it. Traveled-- my parents took separate vacations and family vacations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it was, foremost, a German household. They never denied that they were Jews. They had a Jewish surname, and my father never changed that. But because he had been a member of the cavalry in the First World War, with his own horse and groom-- I remember a painting of him with his helmet under his arm, plumed helmet. He was wounded in Belgium, in Flanders. He had the Iron Cross, first and second class-- therefore, he was German. And that is the way he behaved. He probably never thought of himself much as a Jew. Religion wasn't really his thing. He was almost an atheist. He felt that nature was his church or synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that I remember. That came later. Hitler told me I was Jewish. And my brother and I began to want to go to synagogue. And we celebrated-- we had a menorah, which we never had before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a Christmas tree, and there was a menorah. I, at that time, was old enough not to find it confusing. I was fiercely Jewish by then because I was angry. I remember being angry for so many years. So did you say you and your brother went to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did. I believe we had some religious instruction. But in Germany, religion is taught in schools. And it was certainly then. And we were excused-- when Christian religion was being taught to the others, we were excused. The Jewish kids went somewhere else. And I guess we must have read the Old Testament or something because I don't remember being idle. But when Hitler came to power, things were so-- they started to change so rapidly that I joined an organization, which was apolitical. It was not a Zionist organization. But I remember fiercely clinging to my identity, which is really what everybody has to have to survive all this. So that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of organization was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I really don't remember. All I know is that we wore grey vests. And there were a lot of young kids my age. And we got together, and it was good to belong to something together. But it was not a Zionist organization. They had a goal-- they wanted to emigrate to Israel. That did not happen in our organization. It was more sport-minded, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think we had sports, primarily. And got together and were friends. It's a long time ago. I forgot. But I never belonged to anything before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an ordinary elementary school. And I was looking forward to going to the lyceum, which was not very far away from where we were living. And I barely got started there when I had to leave. I think, altogether, I was in public schools in Berlin for 12 year-- I mean until I was 12. And then I went to a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you have to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Decree, official decree. No more Jews in our schools. It was uncomfortable, all right. And children feel this very intensely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was sent to a Jewish school, and what that meant was crossing Schoneberg Stadtpark, which was a large park and not safe. I remember being scared a lot of the time. People hanging out that were unsavory. And, but-- and I used my bicycle a lot, too, to do that. One of the fun things was getting that bicycle up to our apartment. I would put it on the back wheel and stand it up inside the elevator. I really used my bike a lot. It was-- Berlin was not overly crowded with cars the way it is now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wow, the Jewish school. Well, everybody was getting ready to leave, and that was the primary thing that-- instead of-- OK, for instance, if I had stayed in that lyceum, I would've learned Latin and Greek. In this Jewish school, we learned English and French. We were getting ready to emigrate. Never mind what-- English and French would come in handy. I remember a teacher by the name of Warschawer. I admired him very much. And I think he was sent away and never heard from, while I was still in that school-- the first inkling of someone I knew that disappeared out of my life for no reason. And then, little by little, these things started to creep in-- very difficult to live with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever talk about what was happening when-- with your parents or at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were so conditioned not to discuss things with us. Never was money mentioned at the table, for instance. That was crude. You don't do that. I don't really recall them telling me a lot about what emigration plans they were making. I do know one thing. Much later, in 1939, there were tickets to Los Angeles on a ship that was captured by the Russians right away. It was called the Bremen, and they used it for their troop transports. So my father pinned those tickets on the wall in New York, later, much later. But obviously, we never got on that ship. The plans he was making-- I can't say that he was actively pursuing plans. He belonged to the type of people that thought, in a cultured country like Germany, this can't be happening. It just mustn't be happening. I must be crazy. I can't believe this. I mean, this is the land of Beethoven and Goethe, so therefore it can't be happening. He was in denial. He was, however, picked up twice. And I'll never forget those nights. And he was allowed to return, but they interrogated him and established who he was and would send him back. And they did this at least twice, maybe even more. The night of November 8th, he never came home at all. He rode the subway all night. My mother became more and more obviously disturbed. And I could not count on her for anything. She was quite weak. And I always thought she was the one that didn't want to leave, but that was probably not true, in retrospect. My father's business supplied Hitler with custom-tailored shirts. And so on that infamous night-- I don't want to use the word Crystal Night, it's a misnomer-- they saved his business because that's the Jew that makes shirts for Hitler. So we are not going to smash his store. But he had a branch on Kurfurstendamm, which was the elegant shopping street of Berlin. And that was destroyed that night. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Life went on, and I don't remember-- we sheltered some people that were leaving. But my father never actively started to pursue it until much too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father was picked up, how long was he gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: He would be gone for at least 24 hours, if not 48. It was very hard for my mother. My brother was sent to England when he turned 12, on what we now know as Kindertransport. My father had a youth friend in London that he arranged this with. And my brother received an education in England. He was there a long time-- six years, maybe. Went to school and then, when all the children were evacuated from London, he was evacuated as well. He had a very good education in England. Then, when he came here, he was inducted in the army, American Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about your brother going to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first and for many, many years, nothing. I thought it was important to save the men. I never thought of myself as being in danger. In retrospect, I believe my parents kept me home because I was a very upbeat person. I was very strong emotionally, as well as physically. And they needed me. That really could be all there is to that. I don't want to put any more meaning into it. The three of us went through all that came later together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they talk about why they sent your brother to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I seem to remember because he was a boy, and he would have been taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any decrees that affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, other than not being able to go to the schools of my choice or pursuing a regular lifestyle, not being able to sit on a bench because it said, Juden verboten-- this kind of thing? Or what do you have in mind?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's all around you. You cannot really breathe. It's everywhere. The person who lived in our home-- the cook and woman that lived there, who was my good friend, joined the Nazi party because she could not get away from it. That was just what she had to do. And I think she believed that they were right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed away from political discussions. But a horrific story sticks in my mind. After my brother left-- this must have been end of '38, already beginning '39, Hitler introduced something for the Hitler-- for the young people. They had to come and serve every summer and donate labor to make the fatherland strong or whatever the credo was. And the women came home pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, these were girls my age, and they were 16, 15, 16. And that was the first time the woman across the hall actually acknowledged the misery to my mother. And they cried together and wept over what was happening. We left in May, '39, so I think my father was able to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] buy a visa to France, which was not easy. If you didn't have money, you couldn't do that. He had many dealings, internationally, so-- as a businessman-- and therefore knew a little bit what to do, I'm sure. But he waited way too long. He shouldn't have waited this long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here and change tapes.
